title: Papaya cold-process soap
subtitle: A tropical-scented bar with papaya puree as a partial water substitute and sweet almond oil
excerpt: Papaya puree replaces part of the water in the lye solution, adding natural sugars and a faint golden colour. The oil base is olive, coconut, and sweet almond oil at 5% superfat. A light mango and papaya fragrance is added at trace for an approachable finished bar.
sourceNotes: SAP values: olive oil 0.134, coconut oil 0.190, sweet almond oil 0.136. 5% superfat applied. Fruit puree as partial water substitute in cold-process soap: Susan Miller Cavitch, The Soapmaker's Companion (1997). Sugar content in fruit purees and its effect on lather: Bramble Berry technical notes (2023). Fragrance load at 3% (15 ml per 500 g oil) is within IFRA guidelines for rinse-off skin products.

recipe.batchNotes: Scale by running revised oil weights through a lye calculator. Puree volume scales at the same proportion as the water it replaces.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Blend and freeze the papaya puree the day before. Use frozen or well-chilled; this slows the heat spike when the lye is added.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Cured bars keep two years in a cool dark cupboard. Discard if bars develop orange oily spots or a rancid smell. The fragrance fades after the first year; the bar remains effective.

glossaryTerms.definition: The point at which the soap batter emulsifies to a thick custard. Fragrance oil is added at trace to prevent acceleration.
The percentage of oil left unsaponified in the finished bar for a mild, conditioning result.

content.text: This bar uses blended papaya in the lye solution in place of some of the water. The natural sugars in the fruit puree boost lather volume and contribute a faint golden colour to the finished bar. As with all fruit-puree soaps, the puree should be frozen or well chilled before use; adding it cold to the lye keeps the sugars from scorching and turning the soap brown. Add the fragrance oil at light

items.prepNote: blended smooth and frozen; total liquid 170 ml
3% of oil weight; added at light trace

content.text: Wear safety goggles and chemical-resistant protective gloves in a ventilated space. Have all equipment ready before mixing the lye solution. Keep the frozen papaya puree in the freezer until needed.

Weigh the distilled water into the HDPE pitcher and add the frozen or chilled papaya puree. Add the sodium hydroxide to this cold liquid mixture in a thin, steady stream, stirring continuously. Never add water to lye. The solution heats and gives off fumes; do not lean over the pitcher. Cool to 38 to 42 degrees C.
Melt the coconut oil and combine with the olive and sweet almond oils. Cool to 38 to 42 degrees C.

Pour the lye solution into the oils in a thin stream and stick-blend to light trace. Add the fragrance oil if using, stir in by hand, then pour immediately into the mould. Fruit-puree batches can accelerate quickly; work without delay once the fragrance is added. Cover and wrap in a towel for the gel phase.
Unmould after 48 hours and cut into bars 2.5 cm thick. The bars may be slightly softer than a plain-water soap at this stage; handle with care and leave them on the rack. The
oils and the residual fruit compounds mean the bar softens slightly on contact with water in use; this is normal. Cure for 4 to 6 weeks.

Store cured bars on an open soap dish in a cool dark cupboard. Keeps two years. The fruit-derived colour and fragrance are most vivid in the first 6 months. Discard if bars show orange oily spots or a rancid or sharp smell.
